slides.title: 5. Proposals for ICMP Extensions: Operational Motivations & Rationale
4. foo-over-QUIC: Operational Considerations for when using QUIC as transport protocol
7. Mandating multiple implementations as ingredient for success
8. Raven, an Opensource BGP Routing Validation Tool

chat.author: Mahesh Jethanandani
chat.text: The AIOPS wiki can be found at https://wiki.ietf.org/en/group/ops/aiops

chat.author: Mahesh Jethanandani
chat.text: And the side meeting tomorrow on AIOPS is in Park Suite 4. The room is small, and if you do not find space there, join on WebEx at https://ietf.webex.com/meet/sidemeetings1

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: My recent experience with IP geolocation was that it was used to forbid me from canceling my scheduled milk delivery. Silly me, should have done it before leaving the USA. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

chat.author: Reshad Rahman
chat.text: @John OOC did you try with a VPN?

chat.author: Mahesh Jethanandani
chat.text: @John, it does however show you in "Do not Disturb" mode in the user panel on the right.:smile:

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Could have. Didn’t. Resorted to other expedients.

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: My recent experience with IP geolocation was: download series for offline watching on Netflix. Fly in plane. Land. Try to download more. Can't, because it was a British series and I was now in Britain, where Netflix was not the license holder.

chat.author: Ondřej Caletka
chat.text: I couldn't access some Czech news websites from the IETF network because apparently they applied hard block for packets coming from China - and failed to keep their GeoIP database up to date.

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: This is something we fight all the time with a mobile network.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: But at least we are no more located in Switzerland today

chat.author: Mahesh Jethanandani
chat.text: Is Netflix using geolocation? I thought it was using your IP address. Because I can VPN back to my home country and watch all my episodes.

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: VPN back home worked for this, so I assume it is IP based geolocation

chat.author: Ondřej Caletka
chat.text: What I find funny is that there are more advanced and precise ways of geolocating users, like web browser Geolocation API, but in order to use that, the user has to provide consent, so we rather stuck with something extremely inprecise but without any consent prompt. :)

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: The IETF is always trying to play catch up.  We have a process whereby we update a few GeoIP databases after each meeting to hopefully locate our blocks in the next location.  Clearly, that doesn't work for everyone.  With so many providers, I wonder if the problem is tenable.  Maybe if you could signal a network block is mobile and its location should not be trusted.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: no Valid-Until or Valid-From in the geofeed ?

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: Not in 8805.  But even if we add that, I still foresee lag in the dozen or so operators/sources.  Hence something that would ultimately mark blocks "mobile".  Though I do see that might be abused.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: thx

chat.author: Ondřej Caletka
chat.text: I think the fundamental problem here is that most GeoIP sources work with data files that you as a website operator have to manually download and keep up to date. From my experience with RIPE Meeting network, the GeoIP source databases are fixed within like 2 weeks after geofeed change. But how can you force every single website operator to go and redownload their DB file?

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: Yep!  That's also part of the problem.  Though, we have seen even the databases aren't so fast as two weeks.  For example, to Tommy's point, we do have at least one source reporting us in Spain (heck, one in Barcelona, which seems very wrong).

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: My point is that perhaps if there was a way to mark blocks "mobile", you could still ground them with a registrar (for example, the IETF space would be grounded to RIPE in CH), but services that consume the data would see that we are a mobile network and not offer localization services.

chat.author: Ondřej Caletka
chat.text: But there's issue because eventually the GeoIP blocker has to provide a binary result whether to block. And if the behaviour for "block everything from abroad" would be to block mobile addresses, we have a problem. If it does not block them, then everybody will start abusing it and eventually the service operators will change behaviour to the former one. :)

chat.author: Mohit Tahiliani
chat.text: Linux QUIC from netdev 0x1A held last week: https://netdevconf.info/0x1A/sessions/talk/linux-quic-bringing-a-modern-secure-transport-into-the-kernel.html

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text:
For true mobile networks, a default to block might not be the worst thing to do.  At least it would be deterministic.  Today (literally), depending on your IETF block and the service you're using you may or may not be blocked.
One thing this could do is signal that new locations shouldn't be "divined" from other attributes (e.g., seeing other SSIDs), and the location is rooted in a known value.  This way, people that can work with their local ITs could at least give a deterministic location.

chat.author: Benoît Claise
chat.text: forgot to mention; and the mapping for the specific management protocols must happen somewhere here in OPS

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Most importantly, what color should it be? :-P

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: Eric: management over UDP...like SNMP?  Seems like the XBM draft is SSNMP (Super Simple Network Management Protocol).  I'd be willing to help on the AUP.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: @Joe kind of SNMP for operation but simpler and no BER encoding, so SSNMP would be a good acronym

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: No community string, either ;-)

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: Indeed ;-)

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: CBOR encoded of course

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: My point is that ICMP is a control protocol for the IP layer, not to measure the temperature of the router

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: I agree.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: As in ICMP is control plane while SNMP is management plane

chat.author: Jéferson Nobre
chat.text: Regarding a possible NMOP/NMRG joint session, it would be 1 WG and 1 RG. The example provided bh Thomas, BMWG/IPPM, is composed of 2 WGs. Is this a significant difference or it does not matter?

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: @Jeferson, agree, the difference is bigger. You mentioned ANIMA and NMRG which did it in the past. From that experience, would you say that it is worth a try?

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: The thing I added myself to the queue to say 1 ms after Med locked it :-) was that one possibly unintended consequence of this way of working is that one larger spec may become several smaller specs, as different severable parts get implemented at different rates. You can view this as a bug or a feature but it’s a fact. And it means that sometimes this practice results in greater, not less, RFC production overhead.

chat.author: Mohamed Boucadair
chat.text: Sorry Ahmed  Thomas, we need to leave some time for Ritesh

chat.author: Mahesh Jethanandani
chat.text: Sorry Ahmad and Thomas. We had to cut the queue to give time to the last presentation.

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: To the previous point, sometimes the magic AI fairy helps. Sometimes, not, particularly in the case of specs that don’t stand alone but rather have to be implemented in the context of some (much) larger and higher-stakes technology. Not necessarily ones that need silicon to be made, although it could be.

chat.author: Alex Huang Feng
chat.text: Implementations in OPS have been missing. And sometimes, in some RFC were published and were just not implementable. So changing this policy may help a lot.

chat.author: Jéferson Nobre
chat.text: Thomas, I think the difference regarding ANIMA and NMRG is that NMRG served as a landing space for the I-Ds which led to ANIMA. Besides that, NMRG held specific meetings for autonomics. But after the BoF, there were not joint sessions or aligned docs.

chat.author: Jéferson Nobre
chat.text: In any case, I believe it is worh trying.

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: @job, I suggest to consider a different approach. I believe that documents with existing running code should be preferred over documents which don't. That process starts already when the documents are still individual documents. We as chairs have it in our hands to encourage the document authors to do implementations, even prefer documents when allocacting session time and working group adoption. Reserve time on agenda for hackathon presentation, where a document was implemented or interoparability was being tested.

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: @job, once the working group realizes that documents having implementations being fast tracked, things will change. Thoughts?

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Carrot, not stick?

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: Exactly!

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: the stick comes after

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: AS_SET is deprecated, essentially for this reason

chat.author: Thomas Graf
chat.text: another example... reserve 15 minutes on your agenda for hackathon activities and ask in all presentation to state at the end wherever there is an implementation where proposed changes have been implemented.

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text:
In any case, I believe it is worh trying.

I agree.  @Thomas, the combined meeting is worth a try.
